content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 16 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 108v–108r, LC PDF pages 213–214 | **Source Images:** DEU_016_page_001.jpg, DEU_016_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0153–pa_0154 — DEU_016_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, DEU_016_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Guard the month of Aviv [ha-Aviv] and make the Passover [Pesach] to the LORD your God, for in the month of Aviv the LORD your God brought you out from Egypt by night.

**2** And you shall sacrifice the Passover to the LORD your God, from the flock and the herd, in the place which the LORD will choose to make his name dwell.

**3** You shall not eat with it anything leavened [chametz]; seven days you shall eat with it unleavened bread [matzot], the bread of affliction—for in haste you went out from the land of Egypt—so that you may remember the day of your coming out from the land of Egypt all the days of your life.

**4** And no leaven shall be seen with you in all your territory for seven days, and none of the flesh that you sacrifice on the evening of the first day shall remain overnight until morning.

**5** You are not permitted to sacrifice the Passover in one of your gates which the LORD your God is giving you.

**6** But at the place which the LORD your God will choose to make his name dwell, there you shall sacrifice the Passover in the evening, at the going down of the sun, at the appointed time of your going out from Egypt.

**7** And you shall cook and eat [it] in the place which the LORD your God will choose, and you shall turn in the morning and go to your tents.

**8** Six days you shall eat unleavened bread, and on the seventh day is a solemn assembly [atzeret] to the LORD your God; you shall not do any work.

**9** Seven weeks you shall count for yourself; from [the time] the sickle begins [to cut] on the standing grain you shall begin to count seven weeks.

**10** And you shall make the Feast of Weeks [Chag ha-Shavuot] to the LORD your God—a freewill offering of your hand that you shall give, as the LORD your God blesses you.

**11** And you shall rejoice before the LORD your God, you and your son and your daughter and your male servant and your female servant and the Levite who is in your gates and the sojourner [ger] and the fatherless [yatom] and the widow [almanah] who are in your midst, in the place which the LORD your God will choose to make his name dwell.

**12** And you shall remember that you were a slave in Egypt, and you shall keep and do these statutes.

**13** The Feast of Booths [Chag ha-Sukkot] you shall make for yourself for seven days, when you gather in from your threshing floor and from your winepress.

**14** And you shall rejoice in your feast—you and your son and your daughter and your male servant and your female servant and the Levite and the sojourner and the fatherless and the widow who are in your gates.

**15** Seven days you shall keep the feast to the LORD your God in the place which the LORD will choose, because the LORD your God will bless you in all your yield and in all the work of your hands, and you shall only rejoice.

**16** Three times a year all your males shall appear before the LORD your God in the place which he will choose: at the Feast of Unleavened Bread and at the Feast of Weeks and at the Feast of Booths; and they shall not appear before the LORD empty-handed.

**17** Every man [according to] the gift of his hand, according to the blessing of the LORD your God which he has given to you.

**18** Judges and officers [shofetim ve-shotrim] you shall appoint for yourself in all your gates which the LORD your God is giving you, according to your tribes, and they shall judge the people with righteous judgment [mishpat tsedek].

**19** You shall not pervert justice; you shall not show partiality; and you shall not take a bribe, for the bribe blinds the eyes of the wise and twists the words of the righteous.

**20** Righteousness, righteousness you shall pursue [Tsedek tsedek tirdof]—so that you may live and possess the land which the LORD your God is giving you.

**21** You shall not plant for yourself an Asherah [tree], any wooden thing, beside the altar of the LORD your God which you shall make for yourself.

**22** And you shall not set up for yourself a pillar [massebah], which the LORD your God hates.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**v.1 — chodesh ha-Aviv:** "The month of Aviv." Aviv (אָבִיב) means "young ears of grain" or "spring." The month-name appears only in the older calendar; post-exilic texts use Nisan. The LC reads chodesh ha-Aviv.

**v.1 — Pesach:** Rendered "Passover" with the Hebrew term preserved at first occurrence. The name Pesach (פֶּסַח) derives from pasach, "to pass over/skip." LXX (p. 153) φάσεκ — a transliteration.

**v.3 — lechem oni:** "The bread of affliction." Literally "bread of poverty/affliction." The matzot is identified with the suffering of Egypt — a sacramental memory. LXX ἄρτον κακώσεως.

**v.7 — u-vashalta ve-akhalta:** "And you shall cook and eat." The verb basal can mean "cook/boil" (so LXX ἑψήσεις) but also "roast" in some contexts. The LC reads u-vashalta which strictly means "boil/cook" — this stands in tension with Exod 12:9 which prohibits boiling and mandates roasting (tsal). Deuteronomy's use of basal may indicate a different tradition or a broader semantic range in Deuteronomic usage. EMET translates literally as "cook" without harmonizing.

**v.8 — atzeret:** "Solemn assembly." The term atzeret (עֲצֶרֶת) denotes a "closing assembly" or "restraint-festival." LXX ἐξόδιον — "a send-off," which is an unusual rendering.

**v.9 — mechel chermesh ba-qamah:** "From [the time] the sickle begins on the standing grain." The Shavuot count begins at the first application of the harvest sickle. This is the Deuteronomic reckoning; Leviticus 23 has a different anchor point (the day after the Sabbath from Passover week). EMET renders the LC literally.

**v.10 — missat nidevat yadekha:** "A freewill offering of your hand" — the gift is proportional and voluntary, not a fixed amount. The Deuteronomic festival tithe is tied to agricultural blessing received.

**v.16 — three times a year:** The three pilgrimage festivals (regalim): Pesach (Unleavened Bread), Shavuot (Weeks), Sukkot (Booths). The phrase "appear before the LORD" (yira'eh el-pene YHWH) uses the same verb as "appear/be seen."

**v.18 — shofetim ve-shotrim:** "Judges and officers." Two distinct roles: shofet (judge/decision-maker) and shoter (officer/enforcer). LXX (p. 154) κριτάς καὶ γραμματοεισαγωγεῖς — "judges and court-scribes/registrars."

**v.19 — ha-shochad ye'aver 'eyne chakhamim:** "The bribe blinds the eyes of the wise." Chakhamim = "the wise" — LC clearly reads chakhamim (not tsaddiqim = righteous as in Exod 23:8, which reads "the eyes of the seeing"). The Deuteronomic text has the harder reading: even the wise are corrupted by bribes.

**v.20 — tsedek tsedek tirdof:** "Righteousness, righteousness you shall pursue." The doubled noun tsedek tsedek is one of the most celebrated phrases in Deuteronomy — a cognate emphatic or chiastic intensification. EMET preserves the doubling literally. LXX (p. 154) δικαίως τὸ δίκαιον διώξῃ — "justly the just you shall pursue" — renders it with adverb + noun rather than noun doubled.

**v.21 — asherah:** "Asherah" preserved. The Asherah was a stylized wooden pole or tree representing the Canaanite goddess Asherah/consort of El. The prohibition to plant (tita') one "beside the altar" targets the syncretistic practice of associating the goddess with the LORD's worship site.

**v.22 — massebah:** "Pillar." Rendered literally with Hebrew term preserved. The standing stone (massebah, מַצֵּבָה) had earlier been a legitimate cultic marker (Gen 28:18; 31:45) but is now prohibited in Deuteronomy. LXX στήλην — "stele/pillar."

**LXX comparison (Vaticanus pp. 153–154):** The Vaticanus text runs closely with the LC throughout ch. 16. Key divergences: (1) v.7 — LXX ἑψήσεις ("boil/cook") aligns with LC basal. (2) v.20 — LXX renders tsedek tsedek with adverb + noun (δικαίως τὸ δίκαιον) rather than noun-doubled. (3) v.8 — LXX ἐξόδιον for atzeret is unusual. Page 154 begins with the justice section (v.18ff).

— *EMET*
